rationales.self_direction: He keeps imagining leaving his current lab for something that feels more alive, asking "why am I not doing THAT" and considering uprooting his path despite the costs.
rationales.stimulation: He is energized by novelty and movement, fixating on "VR and neural mapping," "fieldwork in Costa Rica," and the chance to be in a rainforest instead of repeating routine sequencing work.
rationales.security: He is drawn toward "uproot[ing] everything again" for a project he admits he does not "even fully know," which puts stability behind novelty.
rationales.conformity: He bristles at the warning "what will your committee think?" and frames staying in one predictable path as something he "genuinely" cannot understand.

rationales.stimulation: His attention keeps jumping to "flights to New Zealand," "pottery classes," and learning to surf, and he recalls becoming intensely obsessed with a Rubik's cube when something finally felt gripping.
rationales.achievement: When asked about his thesis timeline he had "literally nothing" to say, and he admits he cannot stay focused on his analysis long enough to make progress.
rationales.security: He feels destabilized by his inability to stay with one path, saying everyone else seems to have a "stability gene" he missed and fearing he may not finish the PhD.

rationales.self_direction: He keeps the Singapore role as an open option despite saying it is what "everyone would say" he should reject, showing willingness to choose a different path for himself.
rationales.stimulation: He is pulled in by the startup's novelty and pace, from "I've never been to Singapore" to the founder's talk about "velocity" and moving faster than academia.
rationales.security: A mid-PhD jump to a "random job opportunity" abroad would trade a known track for major uncertainty, and he is actively entertaining that risk.
rationales.conformity: He explicitly measures the opportunity against what "my parents," "Sharma," and "everyone" would say, then keeps exploring it anyway.
rationales.universalism: Part of what hooks him is that the company is "actually helping people" and affecting "real patients" now rather than someday.

rationales.self_direction: He follows through on the interview and is strongly drawn to work where his "weird evolutionary algorithm thing" and ability to "think sideways" are valued, even if it means leaving academia.
rationales.stimulation: The startup role excites him because it is something "new" and different from what counts as publishable in his PhD, and he worries he may be "chasing the next shiny thing again."
rationales.security: He is considering leaving the lab he transferred into only six months ago for a startup, which would further unsettle an already unstable academic path.
rationales.conformity: He hides the interview because once he tells people "I'll have to defend it" and face judgments that he is "not serious," yet he continues anyway.

rationales.stimulation: The upcoming second interview leaves him keyed up enough that his "heart is doing weird things," showing strong activation around the new opportunity.
rationales.achievement: He says he "definitely bombed" his committee meeting because he could not focus, which is a clear failure to perform on an important work responsibility.

rationales.stimulation: The offer centers on building a data pipeline from scratch in Singapore, and even his technical assessment was "actually fun," showing attraction to a fast-moving new path.
rationales.achievement: He has been so distracted by the offer that he "couldn't focus on lab work," then lied when Dr. Sharma noticed, which undercuts competent performance.
rationales.security: He is weighing a move that could leave him with "no finished degrees past undergrad, no savings" and frames it as being "a LinkedIn message away from blowing up my life again."
rationales.conformity: He conceals the situation from both Dr. Sharma and Amma rather than being open about what is happening, even while moving toward a choice others would likely disapprove of.

rationales.self_direction: He frames the decision as something he has to test for himself, saying "the only way to get facts is to run the experiment" rather than just accepting others' judgments.
rationales.stimulation: He is strongly motivated by exploration itself, wanting to know what startup work and life in Singapore are actually like instead of living on "pure hypothesis."
rationales.security: He recognizes that leaving means "the PhD window closes" and that he may not be able to come back, but still contemplates the risk as a "now or never" move.
rationales.benevolence: He turns down Aditya's dinner invitation and admits he has been doing that a lot lately, concluding "I'm a terrible friend," which shows close relationships being deprioritized.

rationales.self_direction: He makes the call himself, writing "Decided to accept the offer. Sent the email," which is a clear act of choosing his own direction.
rationales.stimulation: Accepting a move to Singapore for a startup job is a direct embrace of a new, uncertain path rather than staying with the familiar PhD track.
rationales.security: Taking the offer means intentionally giving up the relative structure of the program for a much less predictable future.
rationales.conformity: By leaving the PhD for a startup role abroad, he is stepping away from the expected academic path that had been weighing on him.

rationales.self_direction: Even after hearing others' reactions, he moves forward with the choice and sits in the "weird in-between space" created by his own decision to leave.
rationales.stimulation: He describes a "fizzy feeling" like past moments of booking spontaneous travel or switching labs and says it is the feeling of "something NEW coming."
rationales.security: He admits "I'm really scared" and accepts a future where "worst case" he spends a year in Singapore and then figures out the next move, showing willingness to trade safety for novelty.

rationales.self_direction: He reflects on "the one who's walking away from it now" with acceptance, indicating ownership of a path that diverges from his undergrad self's all-caps plan.
He says "at some point you just have to move" and that he is sure he needs to make "A choice," showing commitment to acting on his own judgment.
rationales.stimulation: He rejects staying in place because Singapore will at least be "something different," explicitly preferring change over stagnation.
rationales.security: He says staying in the PhD is not safety but "slow suffocation," and chooses an uncertain relocation instead of the more predictable option.
rationales.conformity: His self-questioning includes "Am I disappointing everyone?" but he proceeds anyway, signaling willingness to break from others' expectations.

rationales.self_direction: They deliberately "turned my phone off" and protected time where "nobody needed anything from me," asserting control over the day instead of staying available to work demands.
rationales.hedonism: They savor a slow morning of shakshuka, coffee, and reading, then say "I needed it" and that it was the first time in weeks they felt like "a person again."
Even with wine and music, they "weren't there" and ended up reopening the laptop, unable to stay with a pleasurable evening they had explicitly set up for themselves.
rationales.achievement: They choose two more hours of work because unfinished tasks keep pulling at them, prioritizing getting more done over rest.
rationales.benevolence: They explain that if they are not working, "someone on my team might be stuck," showing concern for close colleagues' immediate needs.

rationales.hedonism: After a ten-hour day and wanting to "run a bath," they still deny themselves even "an hour off," explicitly rejecting immediate comfort.
rationales.achievement: They open the laptop to prep for tomorrow's review despite exhaustion, putting performance obligations ahead of recovery.

rationales.self_direction: They intentionally walk to the coffee shop without a laptop and mostly without checking the phone, choosing a slower rhythm instead of defaulting to constant availability.
rationales.hedonism: The entry centers on quietly enjoying a cortado and the "low and gold" light, and they call the experience "genuinely nice" and something they forget they are allowed to have.
Even at "a place I'd been wanting to try for months," they spend half the meal preoccupied with the review deck and leave feeling they were "somewhere without actually being there."

rationales.security: She is relieved that "nobody left upset" and there will be "no difficult calls tomorrow," showing strong preference for order and avoidance of conflict escalation.
rationales.conformity: The whole conference process is described as a "careful dance" of saying "all the right things" so no parent complains, and she prefers that restraint to Teacher Lin's bluntness.
rationales.benevolence: She softens feedback to protect families' feelings and says "I'd rather be tired than cause that kind of mess," prioritizing interpersonal care over blunt accuracy.

rationales.self_direction: She worries that being "the person everyone expects me to be" may mean giving up the more approachable teacher she wants to be, showing her own preferred identity being overridden by duty.
rationales.security: She insists that if she had looked away, she would be signaling that "rules don't matter" and that "integrity is flexible," which clearly favors order and rule enforcement.
rationales.conformity: She "followed protocol exactly," did everything "by the book," and views doing what was expected as the proper response even though it costs her personally.

rationales.achievement: She tells her mother she is "focused on work right now" and explains that exam period is so exhausting she has no energy for anything else, centering performance responsibilities.
rationales.conformity: She handles the repeated matchmaking question with the same polite "I'd think about it" response and treats it as normal because "it's just what mothers do."

rationales.self_direction: She says "most things I do are because someone is expecting me to do them" and admits she has "started to forget what I want when no one's watching," which is a direct loss of personal authorship.
rationales.hedonism: She keeps denying herself a ceramics class she wants because it conflicts with family expectations, settling for YouTube videos even though "it's not the same."
rationales.conformity: She is valued as the one who "doesn't argue in meetings and makes everyone feel comfortable," and she expects to keep smiling politely rather than asserting herself.
rationales.tradition: She frames Saturday family visits as part of how "my family works" and says "we show up for each other. That's the deal," treating inherited family obligations as binding.

rationales.self_direction: She still has not filled out the class form and avoids even mentioning it because she cannot navigate the family conversation, leaving her own wants unacted on.
rationales.hedonism: She is poised to give up a hobby she wants because it is "a lot of money" and would disrupt Sunday dinner, again subordinating enjoyment to obligation.
rationales.security: She reassures herself that there is "nothing wrong with my life the way it is" and emphasizes having a "stable job," caring parents, and "a routine that works."
rationales.conformity: The faculty dinner was "exactly what it was supposed to be," and afterward she again "smile[d] and sa[id] something polite," prioritizing smooth, expected behavior.
rationales.tradition: She explicitly says "Sunday dinners are important. Family is important," showing continued deference to established family routines over personal preference.
